description: Fire is the first deity the Rig Veda calls upon, and the Agni Chalisa keeps that priority alive. Its forty chaupais (chalis meaning forty) honour Agni Deva, with the framing dohas rounding the recitation to forty-three verse-units. The text grows out of the Vedic Agni worship tradition carried through the Rig and Yajur Vedas. Households who perform havan or yajna turn to it because reciters say it enhances the fruit of every fire ritual. Beyond that, the chant is held to purify body, mind, and surroundings through the grace of the divine fire, and to strengthen Jatharagni, the digestive fire that keeps the body in good health. Sunday (Ravivar) is the day set aside for it. On this page sits the full Devanagari text alongside Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF, a roughly seven-minute read across its verses.

Lakshmi is rarely worshipped as a single goddess; she arrives in eight forms, and the Ashta Lakshmi Dhyanam meditates on every one of them. This traditional eight-verse prayer asks not for one kind of fortune but for prosperity that touches every corner of life at once. Devotees recite it to draw the blessings of courage, wisdom, wealth, and progeny, and the older tradition also calls on it to win victory over enemies and obstacles. Friday (Shukravar) is its weekly home, with Diwali and Varalakshmi Vrata its two great occasions. The recitation runs about eight minutes, short enough to fold into a daily routine. The complete Dhyanam appears here in Devanagari, with plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a downloadable PDF for offline chanting.

A thousand names of Lord Ayyappa, drawn from the Dharma Shastra Purana, fill the Ayyappa Sahasranama Stotram. Pilgrims preparing for Sabarimala lean on it especially during Mandala Kalam, the forty-one-day vrat, when its power is said to be at its height. The recitation invokes those thousand names and their blessings, and the tradition holds that it washes away every sin and purifies the soul, carrying the devoted reciter toward moksha, eternal liberation. Saturday (Shanivar) is the day kept for it. At roughly thirty minutes across its fifteen verses, it suits both an unhurried daily practice and the longer devotions of the pilgrimage season. Here you will find the full stotram in Devanagari, an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF to download.

When a home, temple, or village needs guarding against theft and crime, devotees of the fierce Bhairava reach for the Bhairav Chalisa. Its forty chaupais (chalis, forty) give the form its name, while the bracketing dohas bring the full count to forty-three verse-units; this edition runs to forty-two verses. The text comes from the traditional Chalisa Sangrah. Reciters credit it with strong protection against evil spirits, black magic, and negative energies, and turn to it specifically to lift Kalsarpa Dosha and the malefic pulls of Rahu, Ketu, and Shani. It also instils fearlessness and victory over enemies. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, Kaal Bhairav Ashtami its festival, and the recitation takes about fifteen minutes. The page carries the Devanagari, transliteration, Hindi meaning, and a free PDF.

Her name means queen of the universe, and the Bhuvaneshwari Stotram addresses exactly that: the fourth of the ten Mahavidyas, sovereign over the entire cosmos. Preserved in the Tantrasara, these eight verses ask for mastery over the three worlds and the fulfilment of every desire. Reciters seek through them the gifts of vidya or knowledge, along with wealth, fame, dharma, and moksha. The classical tradition also turns to the stotram to dispel fear in those who take refuge in the goddess. Tuesday and Friday are the days marked for it, with Navratri and Purnima its festivals. Five minutes carries you through all eight verses. On this page the complete text waits in Devanagari, paired with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free downloadable PDF.

Anyone wrestling with anxiety, depression, or a restless mind has long been pointed toward the Chandra Sahasranama Stotram, which Hindu tradition associates with exactly those troubles of the heart. Found in the Skanda Purana, this hymn names the moon among the Navagraha across twenty-three verses. Reciters chant it to clear Chandra dosha and other planetary afflictions, to support eye health and ease eye-related diseases, and to settle the mind into peace and emotional stability. Monday (Somvar) is its weekly day, while Sharad Purnima and Kartik Purnima mark its high points in the calendar. The full recitation lasts around twenty-five minutes. You can read it all here in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF for offline use.

Ten goddesses, one hymn. The Dasha Mahavidya Stotram gathers all ten Mahavidya forms of the Supreme Goddess into fifteen verses drawn from the Tantra Shastra. Practitioners recite it for complete spiritual knowledge, reaching toward sarvajnatva, omniscience itself, and for the incomparable wealth, power, and prosperity the ten forms are said to confer. The tradition also invokes it to clear away every enemy through the combined grace of the Mahavidyas. Three days carry it through the week, Tuesday, Saturday, and Friday, and three festivals raise it higher still: Navratri, Kali Puja, and Diwali. Twelve minutes covers the recitation. This page holds the complete stotram in Devanagari, a plain-ASCII transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for chanting away from the screen.

Adi Shankaracharya wrote these twelve verses as an apology, and that is what makes the Devi Aparadha Kshamapana Stotram unusual: it asks the Divine Mother to forgive every offence committed against her. Where most hymns request blessings, this one seeks pardon. Reciters return to it to feel the unconditional motherly love and compassion of Devi, to set down the guilt and spiritual burden left by past mistakes, and to deepen the bond between devotee and goddess. Tuesday and Friday are the days for it, Navratri the festival. The whole composition takes about eight minutes to recite through its dozen verses. On this page the text appears in full Devanagari, accompanied by an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

Ayurveda traces its divine source to Dhanvantari, the physician of the gods, and this set of mantras invokes him directly for healing. Gathered from the Bhagavata Purana and the Ayurvedic tradition, the ten verses bundle together the Mula Mantra, the Gayatri, and the Bija Mantra so that health worship is complete in one sitting. Reciters chant it to clear away disease through the grace of the divine physician, and to win longevity along with freedom from old age, illness, and the fear of death. The whole intent is holistic healing of body and mind. Saturday (Shanivar) is its day, Dhanteras its festival. Seven minutes carries the recitation. Here you can read the complete text in Devanagari, follow the transliteration, study the Hindi arth, and download a free PDF.

Some prayers ask; this one armours. The Durga Kavach, drawn from the Durga Saptashati in the Markandeya Purana, wraps the reciter in an impenetrable divine shield. Across its eighteen verses the Matrikas are stationed to guard all ten directions, turning the chant into living protection against enemies, evil spirits, and negative energies, and a source of victory in every battle and conflict. Three days hold it through the week, Tuesday, Friday, and Saturday, and Navratri lifts it to its yearly peak. Ten minutes sees you through the full kavach. On this page the complete text stands in Devanagari beside a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download to recite the armour wherever you are.

Rudra is not one but eleven, and the Ekadasha Rudra Stotram praises each of those eleven forms in turn, naming the distinct attributes of every one. It belongs to the Shiva Purana and the wider Rudra tradition, and is counted among the rarest and most potent of Shaiva hymns, sudurlabha, hard to come by. Across eleven verses it is held to grant victory over maha mrityu, the great death, and a long life, to purify the reciter of every sin, and to lead the soul to Rudra Loka. Monday (Somvar) is its day; Maha Shivaratri, the month of Shravan, and Pradosh are its sacred times. Eight minutes covers the recitation. Find it here in full Devanagari with transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download.

Short enough to chant in three minutes, the Ganesha Hridayam carries the heart-essence of devotion to Ganesha in just five verses. It comes down through the Purana tradition. Reciters take it up for spiritual growth and to draw the blessings of Ganesha in all his forms, calling on his divine protection and the auspiciousness he brings. The tradition also holds that it removes sins and bestows spiritual merit on the one who recites it. Wednesday (Budhvar) is the day kept for it, with Ganesh Chaturthi its festival. Because it is so brief, it slips easily into a daily routine. On this page you will find the complete Hridayam in Devanagari, a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Anyone who performs the daily Sandhya Vandana finds a natural companion in the Gayatri Chalisa, a hymn to Goddess Gayatri built from forty chaupais (chalis, forty) with framing dohas that bring the total to forty-three verse-units. It is preserved in the traditional Chalisa Sangrah and runs to forty-two verses here. Reciters credit it with sharpening intellect, wisdom, and spiritual knowledge, with purifying the mind and granting clarity of thought, and with washing away the sins and negative karma gathered across many lifetimes. Sunday and Thursday are its days, and Gayatri Jayanti its festival. About fifteen minutes carries the recitation. The page presents the full text in Devanagari, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF you can download.

Recited at dawn, day after day, the Gopala Sahasranama Stotram is said to fulfil the heart's desires, a thousand names of Gopala Krishna pouring out across twenty verses. The hymn is drawn from the Brahmanda Purana. Devotees turn to it for the immense spiritual merit and blessings it carries, and the tradition links it closely with prosperity, peace, and happiness in the home. Wednesday and Friday are its weekly days, and three festivals raise it higher: Janmashtami, Holi, and Govardhan Puja. The recitation of all thousand names takes roughly thirty minutes. Here you can read the complete stotram in Devanagari, follow along with the English transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF for chanting offline.

Grishneshwar is the twelfth and last of the Jyotirlingas, and reciting its stotram is held to equal a pilgrimage to that shrine, completing the merit of visiting all twelve. The text belongs to the Shiva Purana. Across its eleven verses it is said to grant every siddhi and spiritual accomplishment, while the tradition holds that it removes all sins and brings total purification to the reciter. Monday (Somvar) is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar its appointed times. Eight minutes carries you through the whole recitation, easily fitted into a daily round of worship. On this page the complete Grishneshwar Stotram appears in Devanagari, paired with a plain transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Drawn from the Hanuman Samhita, the Hanuman Hridayam distils devotion to Hanuman into nine compact verses. Reciters take it up first of all for courage, strength, and fearlessness, the qualities Hanuman embodies. The classical tradition also invokes it to drive out disease and grant perfect health, to clear away every enemy and obstacle in life, and to bestow wisdom, eloquence, and a sharp intellect. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, Hanuman Jayanti its festival. The whole hymn takes about seven minutes to recite, brief enough for daily practice. On this page you will find the complete Hridayam in Devanagari script, with an English transliteration running alongside, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline chanting.

Eight verses for the king of the gods. The Indra Ashtakam praises Indra as ruler of the Devas and lord of heaven, picturing him with his Vajra thunderbolt and his great elephant Airavata. Its roots run back to the Rig Veda and the wider Puranic tradition, making it a Vedic hymn for overcoming obstacles and defeating enemies. Reciters chant it for victory, strength, leadership, and authority, the very powers Indra commands. Thursday (Guruvar) is the day set aside for it. Just four minutes carries you through all eight verses, so it slots neatly into a daily routine. Here the complete Ashtakam appears in Devanagari, accompanied by a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for reciting away from the screen.

To recite the Jagannath Stotram is, by tradition, to stand before the Lord of the Universe himself, the hymn carrying merit equal to a pilgrimage to Jagannath Puri Dham and to the darshan of Lord Jagannath. It comes from the Skanda Purana, specifically the Purushottama Kshetra Mahatmya. Across ten verses it invokes that compassionate presence and is said to remove all sins and bestow Vishnuloka on the reciter. Thursday and Sunday are its days, while Rath Yatra, Janmashtami, and Ekadashi mark its festivals. Eight minutes covers the recitation, suiting both daily worship and special occasions. On this page the full stotram is laid out in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline use.

Fierce and protective, Kali answers to a thousand names, and this Sahasranama Stotram from the Kalika Purana recites every one. Across twenty verses the reciter invokes those names and the powers behind them, seeking the protection the goddess is famed for, a strong guard against enemies, evil spirits, and black magic. The tradition holds that it removes every sin, both maha-papa and upa-papa, and that it grants sarva-siddhi, the full range of spiritual accomplishments. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, and four festivals raise it through the year: Navratri, Kali Puja, Diwali, and Amavasya. The full recitation runs about thirty-five minutes. Here you can read the complete stotram in Devanagari, follow the transliteration, study the Hindi arth, and download a free PDF for chanting offline.

Twelve names of Kartikeya, set down for morning recitation, form the heart of this short stotram from the Skanda Purana. Devotees of Murugan and Subrahmanya keep it close, chanting its five verses at the start of the day. The tradition holds that it fulfils desires and clears obstacles from one's path, that it removes enemies and cures diseases, and that it bestows knowledge, strength, and fame. Tuesday and Thursday are its days, with Skanda Shashti and Kartik Purnima its festivals. Only three minutes carries you through all five verses, making it an easy daily habit. On this page the complete Dwadash Nama Stotram appears in Devanagari, alongside a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

This is Rama the archer, bow drawn, and the Kodanda Rama Stotram calls on exactly that warrior form. Preserved in the Agastya Samhita, its nine verses are turned to whenever enemies and opponents must be overcome, a hymn the tradition holds powerful for destroying adversaries through the force of Rama's arrows. Reciters use it for legal battles, competitions, and any confrontation with foes, the protection of those arrows wrapped around them. Sunday (Ravivar) is its weekly day, with Ram Navami and Dussehra its festivals. Five minutes covers the recitation of all nine verses. On this page you will find the complete stotram in Devanagari, an English transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for reciting offline.

Wealth has a guardian, and the Kubera Mantra is the fullest way to approach him: a collection that gathers all the major Kubera mantras for prosperity into one practice. Drawn from the Kubera Tantra, its six verse-units each carry a distinct charge. The Mula Mantra calls on Kubera directly to grant wealth and grain; the Kubera Gayatri confers the wisdom to manage that wealth; and the Bija Mantra activates his energy through seed syllables. Thursday and Friday are its days, while Dhanteras, Diwali, and Akshaya Tritiya are the festivals that frame it. Around ten minutes carries the full recitation. The page presents the complete text in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline chanting.

Five jewels, no more. Adi Shankaracharya composed the Lakshmi Narasimha Pancharatnam as exactly that, five verses, each one a complete act of sharanagati, total surrender at the feet of Narasimha. From the Advaita Vedanta tradition, it counts among the supreme protection hymns. The tradition holds that reciting it removes fear, sorrow, delusion, lust, anger, and all sins, each verse offering the whole self to the man-lion form of Vishnu. Saturday (Shanivar) is its day, with Narasimha Jayanti its festival. Just four minutes carries you through all five gems, brief enough for daily recitation. On this page the complete Pancharatnam appears in Devanagari, paired with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF you can download to recite offline.

At Ujjain stands Mahakaleshwar, the Lord of Time, and the Mahakal Ashtakam is addressed straight to him. These eight verses, from the Shiva Purana, count among the twelve Jyotirlinga prayers and carry immense spiritual merit. Reciters turn to them above all to lift the fear of untimely death, akaal mrityu, and to find freedom from the very cycle of time, kala itself. Two days hold it through the week, Monday and Saturday, while Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan are its festivals. Five minutes covers the full recitation. On this page the complete Ashtakam is set out in Devanagari, with a plain transliteration running alongside, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for chanting away from the screen.

From the Taittiriya Aranyaka comes the Mantra Pushpam, the flower of mantras, a Vedic offering of five verses associated here with Vishnu. Reciters take it up for spiritual growth and to draw the blessings of the Vedic deity in all forms, calling on divine protection and auspiciousness. The tradition also holds that it removes sins and bestows spiritual merit. Unusually, every day of the week suits it, Sunday through Saturday, with Diwali and Ganesh Chaturthi marking its festivals. Four minutes carries you through all five verses, easily folded into any daily practice. On this page the complete Mantra Pushpam is presented in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

No hymn is more closely tied to conquering the fear of death than the Mrityunjaya Stotram, a traditional Sanskrit composition in the Shiva Purana tradition. Across ten verses it is held to prevent untimely death, apamrtyu, and the accidents that bring it. The classical tradition also invokes it to drive out disease and grant health and longevity, and to wash away great sins and negative karma. Monday (Somvar) is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan its sacred times. Seven minutes carries the recitation, brief enough for daily use. On this page you can read the complete stotram in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration line by line, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF for offline chanting.

Shiva as the cosmic dancer, frozen mid-step in creation and dissolution, is the vision the Nataraja Stotram opens up. The hymn belongs to the traditional Sanskrit corpus of the Chidambaram lineage, and its six verses invoke that dancing form. Reciters seek through it artistic talent and mastery in dance and music, fitting for a deity who dances the universe into being. The tradition also holds that it cuts the cycle of birth and death, janana-bhanjana, and grants the vision of that cosmic dance itself. Monday (Somvar) is its day, with Maha Shivaratri, Arudra Darshanam, and the month of Shravan its festivals. Six minutes covers the recitation. Here the full text waits in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download.

Omkareshwar, the fourth Jyotirlinga, sits on an island shaped like the syllable Om, and reciting its stotram is held equal to a pilgrimage there. The text comes from the Shiva Purana. Across ten verses it carries the merit of chanting the pranava, Om, without ceasing, and the tradition holds that the mere darshan of the lingam removes all sins. For the devoted, it promises shiva-sayujya, union with Shiva, after death. Monday (Somvar) is its weekly day, with Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar its appointed times. Eight minutes sees you through the full recitation. On this page the complete Omkareshwar Stotram appears in Devanagari, accompanied by a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download for offline use.

Body part by body part, the Parvati Kavacham shields the reciter, each of Devi's many names guarding a different limb. This eight-verse armour prayer comes from the Shiva Purana and is traditionally invoked to raise the protective armour of Goddess Parvati around the one who chants it. Married women turn to it especially for saubhagya, good fortune in marriage, and reciters more widely seek through it progeny, wealth, and the fulfilment of every desire. Monday and Friday are its days, while Navratri, Gauri Tritiya, and Hartalika Teej mark its festivals. Eight minutes carries the full kavacham. On this page the complete text is laid out in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for reciting the armour offline.

The first moments of waking set the tone for the whole day, and the Pratah Prarthana gathers the essential morning prayers for exactly that purpose. Drawn from various scriptures and associated with Vishnu, its twelve verses move through a complete dawn practice. The reciter invokes the blessings of Lakshmi, Saraswati, and Govind on first opening the eyes, seeks the Earth's forgiveness before the first step touches the ground, and closes with the Pratar Smaranam of Shankaracharya, which lifts consciousness toward Brahman. Eight minutes carries the whole set of prayers through its twelve verses, an unhurried way to begin the morning. On this page you will find the complete Pratah Prarthana in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Rahu is a shadow with no body, and when its dosha or mahadasha presses hard, the Rahu Dosh Nivaran Stotram is the recourse. Taken from the Graha Shanti Vidhi, these seven verses set Durga Devi as the chief remover of Rahu-dosha, pacifying the shadow planet for astrological relief. Reciters chant it to clear the dosha and the afflictions of a Rahu mahadasha, and to dispel the confusion, deception, and sudden misfortunes that Rahu is blamed for. Saturday (Shanivar) is the day kept for it. Just four minutes carries you through all seven verses, so it sits easily within a daily routine. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a plain transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline chanting.

The whole sweep of the Ramcharitmanas, condensed. The Ramayana Chopai distils the essence of Goswami Tulsidas's epic into twenty selected verses, so that reciting them is said to carry the fruit of reading the full Ramayana in shortened form. Devotees take it up for spiritual wisdom and deepening devotion to Rama, and the tradition holds that it lifts ignorance, sorrow, and fear from the heart. Sunday and Thursday are its days, with Ram Navami and Vivah Panchami its festivals. About fifteen minutes carries the recitation through its twenty chaupais. On this page you can read the complete text in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration alongside, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to recite offline whenever you wish.

Its very name promises the destruction of trouble, sankat-nashan, and the Sankat Nashan Ganesh Stotra is recited to clear every obstacle and calamity from life. The hymn comes from the Narada Purana. Across eight verses it is held to fulfil desires of every kind, knowledge, wealth, progeny, and moksha, and the tradition says that reciting it at the three sandhyas of dawn, noon, and dusk removes all fear of obstacles, with six months of steady practice yielding visible results. Wednesday (Budhvar) is its day, Ganesh Chaturthi its festival. Five minutes carries the recitation. On this page the complete stotra is set out in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Students bent over books, writers chasing a phrase, artists reaching for the right line, all find a patron in the Saraswati Pancharatnam, five jewel-verses of the goddess of wisdom composed by Adi Shankaracharya. The hymn is said to grant poetic ability rivalling Kalidasa himself, and to bestow every form of vidya and eloquence on the one who recites it. Wednesday and Thursday are its days, with Vasant Panchami its festival. Just three minutes carries you through all five gems, brief enough to chant before sitting down to work. On this page the complete Pancharatnam appears in Devanagari, paired with a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

No Satyanarayan Katha or Purnima Vrat feels complete without the Satyanarayan Chalisa, the essential hymn for those observances. Built from forty chaupais (chalis, forty) with framing dohas that round it to forty-three verse-units, it is preserved in the traditional Chalisa Sangrah and runs to forty-two verses here. Reciters chant it to fulfil their wishes and clear obstacles, and to draw wealth, prosperity, and happiness into the family. It is also recommended for griha pravesh, vivah, and other auspicious occasions. Thursday (Guruvar) is its day, with Purnima and the Satyanarayan Puja its festivals. About fifteen minutes carries the recitation. On this page you will find the complete Chalisa in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline use.

Eight auspicious verses, addressed to the planet most feared in the horoscope. The Shani Mangalashtakam sets out to please Lord Shani, and devotees keep its recitation for Saturdays, when its benefit is said to be greatest. Drawn from the Skanda Purana, it is chanted above all to relieve the long burden of Sade Sati and the afflictions of a Shani dasha, and to invoke Shani's blessings over karma and justice, the domains he governs. The tradition holds it up as protection from every Shani-related planetary trouble. Saturday (Shanivar) is its day, with Shani Jayanti and Shani Amavasya its festivals. Just three minutes covers the eight verses. On this page the complete Mangalashtakam appears in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download.

Recited at dawn, the Shatru Nashak Stotram is said to destroy every enemy completely, and chanted on Tuesday and Saturday nights, to free the devotee of foes within seven days. This is a supreme hymn for vanquishing adversaries through the power of Hanuman, drawn from the Hanuman Tantra and Puranic compilation. Across its twelve verses the tradition holds that it drives off bhut-pret-pishach, dakini-shakini, and all graha doshas alike. Tuesday and Saturday are its days, with Hanuman Jayanti its festival. Eight minutes carries the full recitation. On this page you can read the complete stotram in Devanagari, follow the English transliteration line by line, study the Hindi arth, and download a free PDF to recite offline wherever you happen to be.

Om Namah Shivaya, repeated until it carries you, is the engine of the Shiv Panchakshar Dhun, an easy-to-chant devotional dhun built for meditation and kirtan. It belongs to the traditional devotional dhun form, weaving the five-syllable Panchakshara mantra through eight verses that pair Sanskrit lines with Hindi devotional poetry. Reciters use it to settle into a deeply devotional, meditative atmosphere, and to purify both mind and surroundings through repeated chanting. Its open, repetitive shape makes it well suited to group recitation and bhajan sessions. Monday (Somvar) is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan its festivals. Seven minutes carries the dhun through its eight verses. On this page the complete text appears in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download.

Each verse of the Shiva Namavali Ashtakam is packed with the sacred names of Shiva, and reciting those names is held to destroy every sin. Drawn from the Shiva Purana, its eight verses are deliberately easy to memorise and repeat day after day. Devotees chant it for the combined blessings of all the forms of Shiva gathered into one hymn, and the tradition holds that steady recitation leads to Shivaloka after death. Monday (Somvar) is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and Pradosh its festivals. Five minutes carries you through all eight verses, fitting comfortably into a daily round of worship. On this page the complete Ashtakam appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Among the oldest wealth-hymns in the tradition, the Shri Suktam sits in the Khilani, the appendix to the Rigveda Samhita, and its fifteen verses call abundance into the home. Reciters chant it to draw wealth, prosperity, and abundance, and to drive out Alakshmi, poverty itself, from house and life. The hymn is traditionally linked with gold, cattle, and material comforts, and is held to bestow fame, reputation, and social standing on the one who recites it. Three days carry it through the week, Thursday, Friday, and Sunday, with Ekadashi and Diwali its festivals. Ten minutes covers the full recitation. On this page you will find the complete Shri Suktam in Devanagari, an English transliteration alongside, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline chanting.

For a household hoping for harmony in marriage, the Sita Chalisa speaks directly to that wish, a hymn to Goddess Sita built from forty chaupais (chalis, forty) with framing dohas bringing the count to forty-three verse-units. Preserved in the traditional Chalisa Sangrah, it runs to forty-two verses here. Reciters credit it with blessing married life with harmony and happiness, with granting strength and patience through hardship, and with bestowing progeny and family contentment, all under the protective grace of Mother Sita. Friday (Shukravar) is its day, while Sita Navami, Vivah Panchami, and Ram Navami mark its festivals. About fourteen minutes carries the recitation. On this page the complete Chalisa appears in Devanagari, with transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download.

Like all kavachas, this one is armour rather than petition: the Subrahmanya Kavacham stations the various names of Kartikeya over each part of the body to shield it. This traditional seven-verse hymn guards the reciter limb by limb through those divine names. Devotees chant it to clear away enemies and win supreme prosperity, and the classical tradition also invokes it against disease and the fear of evil spirits. Tuesday (Mangalvar) is its day, with Skanda Shashti its festival. Eight minutes carries you through the full kavacham, suiting both daily worship and special occasions. On this page the complete text is laid out in Devanagari, with a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download to recite the armour offline.

The Sun is the source of life, and the Surya Stotram is a full hymn to that source, addressed to Surya for health, vitality, and longevity. Drawn from the Bhavishya Purana, its twelve verses are associated in tradition with heart diseases (hrid-roga), jaundice (harimana), and all fevers. Reciters chant it to wash away sins and to lift the darkness of ignorance from life, and the hymn promises health from Surya, strength from Indra, virility from Chandra, and knowledge from Agni. Sunday (Ravivar) is its day, while Makar Sankranti, Ratha Saptami, and Chhath Puja mark its festivals. Eight minutes covers the recitation. On this page you can read the complete Surya Stotram in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download.

The marriage of the Tulsi plant to Vishnu is among the most tender of household rites, and the Tulsi Vivah Stotram accompanies it. From the Padma Purana, its twelve verses are recited for marital harmony and conjugal bliss, and for the saubhagya, good fortune, that married women seek. The tradition holds that the sacred Tulsi worship it carries purifies sins, while deepening Vishnu-bhakti and spiritual devotion in the one who recites. Thursday and Monday are its days, with Tulsi Vivah, Kartik Purnima, and Devuthani Ekadashi its festivals. Eight minutes carries the recitation through its dozen verses. On this page the complete stotram appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Vishnu came once as a dwarf and grew to stride across the worlds, and the Vamana Stotram holds both pictures at once, invoking the expansive power of Trivikrama who took those three cosmic steps. The hymn is drawn from the Vamana Purana. Across eleven verses it is recited to dissolve pride, ego, and arrogance, the very faults the dwarf-form humbled in Bali, and to gather the merit of charitable giving, dana. The tradition holds that it removes all sins and grants liberation. Thursday (Guruvar) is its day, with Vamana Jayanti and Onam its festivals. Eight minutes carries the recitation. On this page you will find the complete Vamana Stotram in Devanagari, with an English transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline use.

At Hampi stands the Virupaksha temple, and reciting its stotram is held to carry the merit of a pilgrimage there. The text belongs to the Shiva Purana. Across its eleven verses it is said to grant divine vision and spiritual wisdom, to conquer death and bestow longevity, while the tradition holds that it removes all sins and purifies the one who recites. Monday (Somvar) is its weekly day, with Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar its appointed times. Eight minutes carries the full recitation, easily folded into a daily round of worship. On this page the complete Virupaksha Stotram appears in Devanagari, paired with a plain transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF you can download to recite offline.

On a brick at Pandharpur stands Vitthal, hands on hips, and the Vitthal Ashtakam praises that distinctive brick-standing form of Vishnu across eight verses. The hymn belongs to the Varkari tradition, and reciting it draws the devotee into the long lineage of Varkari saints who walked to Pandharpur singing his name. Its blessings are held to include liberation from all sins. Thursday (Guruvar) is its day, with Ashadhi Ekadashi and Kartiki Ekadashi, the two great pilgrimage days, as its festivals. Just three minutes carries you through all eight verses, brief enough for daily recitation. On this page the complete Ashtakam appears in Devanagari, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline chanting.
